question: Why can a Poseidon or Poseidon2 hash computed in a Soroban contract differ from a Noir or JavaScript result, and how should inputs be encoded?

golden.answer: Poseidon-family functions operate on field elements, not arbitrary byte strings. Both sides must use the same field, permutation parameters, width, domain-separation convention, byte order, padding, and byte-to-field conversion. For BN254, an encoded value must be a canonical scalar below the field modulus or be reduced by an explicitly shared rule. A mismatch in any of these choices—including selecting Poseidon versus Poseidon2 or a different state width—produces a different hash; do not assume a library default matches Noir.
keyFacts: Poseidon inputs are field elements, so both implementations must agree on the field and byte-to-field mapping.
Both sides must match permutation parameters, width, domain separation, byte order, and padding.
Values must be canonical for the selected field or reduced under the same explicit rule on both sides.
avoid: Zero-padding alone guarantees identical Poseidon hashes across all libraries.
Poseidon and Poseidon2 are interchangeable when given the same bytes.

corroboration.claim: Poseidon inputs are field elements, so both implementations must agree on the field and byte-to-field mapping.
Both sides must match permutation parameters, width, domain separation, byte order, and padding.
Values must be canonical for the selected field or reduced under the same explicit rule on both sides.

Poseidon and Poseidon2 are interchangeable when given the same bytes.
